name: ARDSNet Low Tidal Volume Ventilation
question: Does low tidal volume ventilation improve outcomes in adults with acute lung injury/ARDS?
population: Adults with acute lung injury or ARDS receiving mechanical ventilation.
primary_outcome: Death before discharge and ventilator-free days at 28 days.
summary: In a multicenter randomized trial, low tidal volume ventilation (6 mL/kg predicted body weight) was compared with traditional tidal volumes (12 mL/kg). The low tidal volume strategy reduced mortality and increased ventilator-free days over the first 28 days.

name: PROSEVA (Prone Positioning in Severe ARDS)
question: Does early, prolonged prone positioning reduce mortality in severe ARDS?
population: Adults with severe ARDS (PaO2/FiO2 <150 on lung-protective ventilation).
summary: Patients with severe ARDS were randomized to early prone sessions (at least 16 hours) or supine positioning. Prone positioning significantly reduced 28-day mortality and improved 90-day survival.

name: TRICC (Transfusion Requirements in Critical Care)
question: Is a restrictive transfusion strategy equivalent or better than a liberal strategy in critically ill patients?
population: Critically ill ICU patients with hemoglobin < 9 g/dL after initial stabilization.
primary_outcome: 30-day all-cause mortality and severity of organ dysfunction.
summary: Restrictive transfusion (transfuse if Hb <7 g/dL) was compared with liberal transfusion (Hb <10 g/dL). Overall 30-day mortality was similar; some less-acutely ill and younger subgroups had lower mortality with the restrictive strategy, and in-hospital mortality was lower with restrictive transfusion.

name: SAFE (Albumin vs Saline in ICU Resuscitation)
question: Does albumin vs normal saline affect mortality in ICU fluid resuscitation?
population: General ICU patients requiring intravascular fluid resuscitation.
summary: Patients were randomized to receive 4% albumin or normal saline for resuscitation. There was no significant difference in 28-day mortality or new organ failure between groups.

name: FACTT (Conservative vs Liberal Fluid Strategy in ALI/ARDS)
question: Does conservative fluid management improve outcomes in acute lung injury?
population: Adults with acute lung injury randomized to conservative vs liberal fluid strategies.
summary: Conservative fluid management did not significantly reduce 60-day mortality compared with a liberal strategy. It improved oxygenation and increased ventilator-free and ICU-free days without increasing shock or dialysis.

name: Early Goal-Directed Therapy (Rivers et al.)
question: Does early goal-directed therapy reduce mortality in severe sepsis and septic shock?
population: Emergency department patients with severe sepsis or septic shock.
summary: Patients receiving a 6-hour early goal-directed therapy protocol before ICU admission had lower in-hospital mortality than those receiving standard therapy. Physiologic targets such as central venous oxygen saturation also improved.

name: ProCESS (Protocol-Based Care for Early Septic Shock)
question: Do protocol-based resuscitation strategies improve outcomes compared with usual care in early septic shock?
population: Emergency department patients with septic shock.
summary: Patients were randomized to protocol-based EGDT, protocol-based standard therapy, or usual care. There were no significant differences in 60-day mortality, 90-day mortality, 1-year mortality, or need for organ support.

name: SMART (Balanced Crystalloids vs Saline in ICU)
question: Do balanced crystalloids improve outcomes compared with saline in critically ill adults?
population: Critically ill adults admitted to ICUs.
primary_outcome: Major adverse kidney events within 30 days (death, new renal-replacement therapy, or persistent renal dysfunction).
summary: Balanced crystalloids were associated with a lower rate of major adverse kidney events compared with saline. Thirty-day mortality was not significantly different between groups.

name: ADRENAL (Hydrocortisone in Septic Shock)
question: Does hydrocortisone reduce mortality in ventilated patients with septic shock?
population: Patients with septic shock receiving mechanical ventilation.
summary: Hydrocortisone (200 mg/day) did not significantly reduce 90-day mortality compared with placebo. Hydrocortisone was associated with faster resolution of shock.

name: APROCCHSS (Hydrocortisone + Fludrocortisone in Septic Shock)
question: Does hydrocortisone plus fludrocortisone improve survival in septic shock?
summary: Hydrocortisone plus fludrocortisone reduced 90-day all-cause mortality compared with placebo in septic shock.

name: CLASSIC (Restricted vs Standard IV Fluids in Septic Shock)
question: Does restricting IV fluids reduce mortality in ICU patients with septic shock?
population: ICU patients with septic shock after at least 1 liter of IV fluid.
summary: Restricted IV fluid therapy did not reduce 90-day mortality compared with standard fluid therapy. Serious adverse events and days alive without life support were similar between groups.

name: CLOVERS (Restrictive vs Liberal Fluids in Sepsis-Induced Hypotension)
question: Does an early restrictive fluid strategy improve outcomes compared with a liberal strategy in sepsis-induced hypotension?
population: Patients with sepsis-induced hypotension after initial fluid resuscitation.
summary: An early restrictive strategy prioritizing vasopressors did not significantly reduce 90-day mortality compared with a liberal fluid strategy. Serious adverse events were similar between groups.
